title: Albert Higgs v. Leshelmaryas Investment Company Limited
text:
COMMONWEALTH OF THE BAHAMAS
IN THE COURT OF APPEAL
SCCivApp. No. 212 of 2024
IN THE MATTER of ALL THAT piece parcel or tract of land situate on the Southwestern Side
of the New Harold Road Reservation in the Western District of the Island of New Providence
aforesaid comprising Ninety-two and Thirty-three Hundredths (92.33) acres more or less and
bounded Northwardly by land the property of the Bahamas Government Northeastwardly by the
New Harold Road Reservation leading to John F. Kennedy Drive Eastwardly partly by land
property of the Air Ministry partly by other land the property of Nassauvian Limited partly by land
formerly leased to the late Hedley Edwards and partly by other land and partly by land reserved
for the Bahamas Geodetic Survey southwardly by Crown Land Westwardly partly by land now or
formerly the property of The Caves Company Limited and partly by land now or formerly the
property of G. A. And S. G. Bostfield which said piece parcel or tract of land has such position
shape mars and dimensions as are shown on the diagram or plan attached to a Certificate of Title
issued by the Supreme Court of the Bahama Islands to Nassauvian Limited on the 23rd day of
February, A.D. 1970 and recorded in the Registry of Records in the City of Nassau in the said
Island of New Providence in Volume 1582 at pages 182 to 185 and is delineated on that part which
is coloured pink on the said diagram or plan.
AND IN THE MATTER of the Partition Act, Chapter 153
B E T W E E N
ALBERT HIGGS on behalf of the Estate of Kenneth McKinney Higgs Sr., Deceased
ALBERT HIGGS on behalf of the Estate of Clothilda Eugenia Higgs, Deceased
Appellants
AND
LESHELMARYAS INVESTMENT COMPANY LIMITED
Respondent
BEFORE: The Honourable Madam Justice Charles, JA
The Honourable Mr. Justice Smith, JA
The Honourable Madam Justice Fraser, JA
APPEARANCES: Ms. Bridget Francis-Butler, Counsel for the Appellants
Mr. Damian Gomez, KC, with Mrs. Lilnique Murphy-Grant, Counsel
for the Respondent
Mr. Timothy Eneas, KC, with Ms. Ava La-Roda, appeared, holding a
watching brief
DATES: 25 September 2025; 22 January 2026; 21 April 2026
*****************************************************

Civil Appeal – Partition of land — Consent to partition — Affidavit evidence — No cross-
examination — Weight of Evidence – Appellate interference with exercise of discretion – Valuation
of parcels — Use and enjoyment of land
The parties, after a long and convoluted litigation history, consented to a partition of the land.
Neither party sought a sale, and they agreed to create a 60-foot road reservation at the northern
boundary, leaving the issue of partition to be determined by the court on affidavit evidence,
including expert reports, without cross-examination.
The matter proceeded before the trial judge on competing affidavit evidence. The trial judge,
exercising his discretion, ordered a partition in accordance with the parties’ entitlement, allocating
separate parcels subject to a road reservation and easement, and directing that a portion of the
Appellants’ land be set aside for a judgment creditor of the Appellant.
The Appellants appealed that decision, contending, namely, that the trial judge erred in his
assessment of the value of the respective parcels and in the resulting partition, including his
findings on value, reliance on use and enjoyment, treatment of valuation factors, reliance on a prior
decision which was set aside, and that the decision was against the weight of the evidence.
Held: The appeal is dismissed. The partition is affirmed. The Appellants are to pay the
Respondent’s costs of the appeal, to be taxed in default of agreement.
The Court found that there was no mandate to partition by reference to the valuation of the
respective shareholdings, and that given the constraints with regard to the evidence presented, this
exercise of discretion by the trial judge was the best that could be done in the circumstances.
The Court also found that the trial judge’s conclusions that: (1) road access would remediate any
disparity in value, and (2) the decades of usage of the land by the Appellants were relevant factors
and were not plainly wrong and ought not to be set aside on appeal.
The Court also concluded that it would be an abuse of process to remit the matter back to the trial
court for reconsideration. As the successful party, the Respondent was awarded its costs of the
appeal.
Bahamasair Holdings Ltd. v. Messier Dowty Inc [2018] UKPC 25, applied
Campbell v. Campbell [2017] EWHC 182, mentioned
Higgs Sr. v. Leshel Maryas Investment Company Limited & Annamae Woodside [2009] UKPC 47,
mentioned
Super Value Food Stores Ltd. v. Yvonne Rahming SCCivApp No. 256 of 2017, applied
2

_____________________________________________________________________________
J U D G M E N T
_____________________________________________________________________________
Judgment delivered by The Honourable Mr. Justice Smith, JA:
INTRODUCTION
1. The subject matter of this appeal is a parcel of land comprising approximately 92.33 acres in
Western New Providence in the vicinity of Tonique Williams Darling Highway.
2. After a long and convoluted litigation history dating back to 1967, the dispute had resolved
itself to a determination of a partition as between the Appellants (“Higgs”) and the
Respondent. Neither party wanted a sale of the land. They consented to the court making an
order for partition.
3. The parties put forward their evidence in affidavit form. These Affidavits included 3 expert
reports. There was no cross-examination. The parties left the decision on the partitioning of
the land to the discretion of, Winder C.J. (“the trial judge”).
4. The trial judge decided to partition the land in accordance with the parties’ entitlement,
namely ¼ to the Respondent and ¾ to the Appellants. The division was as per the plan attached
(see exhibit A at page 11 of this Judgment). Parcel A to the Higgs and Parcel B to the
Respondent, save for a road reservation over which the parties would have an easement.
Further, by agreement of the Appellants, a judgment creditor of the Appellants, one Neely’s
of Nassau (Neely’s), was to have a 9 acre parcel of their land partitioned out of the Northern
portion of their Parcel A.
5. The trial judge also ordered each party to bear their own costs.
6. The Appellants have appealed that decision.
7. For the reasons that appear in this Judgment, I would dismiss this appeal and order the
Appellants to pay the Respondent’s costs of the appeal. Such costs to be taxed by the Registrar
in default of agreement.
THE DECISION OF THE TRIAL JUDGE
8. As background to the Decision of the trial judge, the following four factors should be noted.
9. First, the parties had agreed to create a 60 foot road reservation at the northern boundary of
the property where it meets Tonique Williams Darling Highway. This would ensure the
highest value to each parcel of land.
10. Second, the parties agreed to let the matter be decided on the opposing Affidavits of the parties
and other uncontested evidence. There was no cross examination.
3

11. Third, while the Decision of the trial judge at paragraph 37 of his Decision states that the
property was to be partitioned as follows: “(1) The portion of the Property identified as
Parcel A or 22.55 acres to the Higgs.” This is an obvious error as Parcel A as agreed was
67.78 acres and Parcel B was 22.55 acres. Parcel A was to devolve upon the Appellants
(Higgs) and Parcel B to the Respondent.
12. Fourth, the division of the land was in accordance with a prior order made by Thompson J. in
2006. This order had been set aside on appeal to the Court of Appeal and the Privy Council.
However, the basis upon which the prior order of Thompson J. had been set aside was that it
was premature, in that it required proper evidence upon an application for partition. The
question of partition was therefore referred back to the Supreme Court of the Bahamas.
Interestingly enough, the Privy Council was of the opinion that this might have been a fit case
for the exercise of the power of sale rather than partition (see paragraphs 59 and 60 of the
decision in Higgs Sr. v. Leshel Maryas Investment Company Limited & Annamae
Woodside [2009] UKPC 47). However, the matter had finally resolved itself to an acceptance
of the remaining parties for a partition of the land and not a sale.
13. The hearing of that partition application was conducted by the trial judge solely upon
competing affidavits and other uncontested evidence.
14. The trial judge summarized the respective cases put forward by the parties at paragraphs 9 to
22 of his Decision.
15. The case of the Respondent was basically that it had developed Parcel B and erected a
commercial facility that provided entertainment to the general public in the form of a bowling
alley and other related activities. The Appellants in no way contributed to this. On the
contrary, the Respondent alleged that the Appellants had mined Parcel A over the decades of
their possession. They have thus benefited from the land to the exclusion of the Respondent
and it would be unfair to ignore this in the partitioning of the land.
16. The case for the Appellants was basically that it was the Respondent who extensively mined
Parcel B and has benefited from the occupation of the more valuable portion of land. They
asked the court below:
“… to have regard to the use and enjoyment of the land, the
interest of the parties, its valuation, development and potential
future use … and in light of the valuation in respect to the
acreage occupied by [the Respondent], when compared to the
valuation of the remaining portion of the Property occupied by
the Higgs, it is proposed that [the Respondent’s] ¼ interest in
the land ought not to include more than 13 acres … ” (See
paragraph 18 of the trial judge’s Decision)
4

17. The trial judge then analysed the expert reports provided by the parties at paragraphs 28 to 33
of the Decision. One report was provided by each of the parties and another from a former
defendant to the action, one Annamae Woodside.
18. The trial judge then concluded at paragraph 34 as follows:
“Having given careful and anxious consideration to all of the
attendant circumstances of this case, I am not satisfied that I
should make any division other than the natural one made by
Thompson J. in 2006 giving the ¼ or 22.55 acre Parcel B to
Leshel. The remaining ¾ or 67.78 acre Parcel A ought therefore
to now be granted the Higgs.”
19. As to the differences in the Appellants’ suggested valuation of the parcels, the trial judge was
of the view that this was because of the road access and proximity to commercial activity to
Parcel B. This, he opined, could be remediated by road access. I quote paragraph 35 of the
Decision:
“On the evidence there is a considerable ridge remaining on
Parcel A from which the Higgs may continue their quarrying
activities. I therefore found little merit in the suggestion that the
land in Parcel B is of greater value per acre that (sic) Parcel A.
This could only be as a result of the road access and the
proximity to the commercial activity of the Bowling Alley. This
could be remediated by road access.”
20. Further, the trial judge opined that the Appellants too had significantly benefited from their
long use of the property. This was a further reason to stay with the partition as suggested by
the prior order of Thompson J. in 2006. The trial judge stated at paragraph 36 of the Decision:
“I accepted the assessment of Mr. McKinney KC, for Leshel,
that ‘the Higgs have little if any entitlement to raise the issue of
valuation, having regard to the fact that from 1967 and before,
when the first action started and continuing on to now, they have
been on the property, having been making a living off the
property’. I did not accept the suggestion by the Higgs that their
prior excavation took place at a time when they were the sole
owners of the property and should otherwise not be considered.
As this matter has been in the Court system since the 1970’s
there is little merit in this submission. The interest of
Nassauvian, the predecessor of Leshel, has existed since the
1970’s. Likewise, against this background, I did not accept that
the disputed complaint that Leshel may have violated the
injunction ought to change the outcome.”
5

21. The trial judge therefore ordered that the property be partitioned giving Parcel A to the
Appellants and Parcel B to the Respondent. Further, the trial judge ordered that 9 acres of the
Northern section of Parcel A be partitioned and given to Neely’s (a judgment creditor of the
Appellants).
THE APPEAL
22. The Appellants have listed 7 grounds of appeal as follows:
“1. The Learned Judge misdirected himself in fact and in law as
it relates to the physical value of the respective parcels of land
and therefore erred in awarding to the Respondent Parcel B,
excluding the road reservation at Tonique Williams Darling
Highway.
2. The learned judge failed to make specific findings of fact as to
the value of each share of the parcel land. The court merely
determined a value of one quarter (1/4) interest of the total
acreage. The court’s findings were therefore insufficient to
support the conclusion that each share of each parcel would be
materially equal in share of the whole parcel. The physical
division or partition of the land requires the physical inspection,
measurements calculations and considerations to determine the
specific value of the respective shares.
3. The Learned Judge erred in fact and law by relying on the use
of the land in determining whether substantial injury would
result to the parties by the actual partition of the land.
Substantial injury to a party does not occur simply because an
actual partition would not result in the highest and best use of
the land, if the land is partitioned without taking into account
its value. The Learned Judge failed to distinguish between the
value of the respective shares and the respective entitlement of
the parties.
4. The learned Judge misdirected himself when he concluded
that the increase value of the Parcel B was solely as a result of
the development by the Respondent and opined that the
Appellant ought not to benefit from the said development of the
Respondent. The increased value of the land was based
substantially upon the surrounding developments within the
area including the development of subdivision (High Point), the
implementation and construction of roads and utilities which
are not by the Respondent nor the Appellant.
6

5. That in all the circumstances the learned judge failed to take
into account all the evidence that negatively affected the value
of the land which was apportioned to the Appellant. The
Learned Judge failed to consider the depreciation of the value
of the property due to the emission of raw sewerage by a third
party onto the Southern boundary of the subject property. The
raw sewerage is a hazard to the environment and has damaged
the said land thereby effecting the use and enjoyment of a
substantial portion of the said land.
6. The Learned Judge erred in upholding the 2006 decision of
Thompson J. as she then was, which was subsequently appealed
to the Court of Appeal and set aside, and confirmed by the
Council of the Privy Council.
7. That in all the circumstances the judgment is against the
weight of the evidence.”
23. Having examined the written submissions and after entertaining oral submissions, this appeal
really raises 3 issues for consideration.
24. Grounds 1, 2, 3, 4 and 7 contend that the trial judge was wrong not to use their suggested
valuation of the land in deciding on a partition. In that case, as suggested at paragraph 16
above, the Respondent would only have been entitled to about 13 acres of the land.
25. At Ground 5, the Appellants contend that the trial judge failed to consider the depreciation in
value of the land caused by the Respondent permitting the emission of raw sewerage onto the
land.
26. At Ground 6, the Appellants contend that it was wrong for the trial judge to follow the
suggested partition of Thompson J. in 2006 since this had been set aside by the Court of
Appeal and the Privy Council.
ANALYSIS
Grounds 1,2,3,4 and 7
27. Both parties to this appeal agreed that in deciding on a partition of land, the courts have a
broad and unfettered discretion to apportion the land in a manner that was fair and just in the
circumstances of the case in question.
28. In spite of this, the Appellants sought to contend that the guiding factor in this action should
be an apportionment by value of the respective shareholdings.
29. Although authorities were cited by the Appellants to support this contention, no specific
references were provided for any part of the authorities. When pressed to do so in oral
argument, counsel for the Appellants could not point to any specific authority for their
contention that valuation was the cornerstone for any partition. In fact, while counsel cited an
7

authority which, she alleged, supported her contention, namely Campbell v. Campbell
[2017] EWHC 182, the allegedly relevant portion from paragraph 154 of the judgment was
an insertion of 3 pages from an unidentified and unverified source into the case. Counsel could
proffer no explanation for this misquoted and unverified addition to the case.
30. In the end, the Appellant accepted that the court did have a general and unfettered discretion
to partition in accordance with what was fair and just in all the circumstances. There was no
mandate to partition by reference to the valuation of the respective shareholdings (see
generally Halsbury’s Laws of England 1st ed. vol. 21 at paragraph 1605).
31. In written submissions, Counsel for the Appellants contend that:
“3.1 The apportionment of value in a Partition action should
align with the respective beneficial interest of the co-owners,
unless there are overriding considerations or agreements that
dictate otherwise. Thus, it would not generally be equitable or
justifiable for the title owner with ¼ interest to be apportioned
a greater value than the party with a ¾ interest in the land. In
the present case Leshel was granted 22.55 acres at $300,000.00
per acre with a face value in excess of $6,765,000.00 whereas the
Higgs who are entitled to ¾ interest in the subject property was
granted Parcel B with approximately 67 acres valued at
$50,000.00 to $100,000.00 per acre at greatest value of
$6,700.00.00 (sic) provided we accept the 2010 appraised value
of Robin Brownrigg of Bahamas Realty.”
32. According to Counsel, this was not fair and just.
33. However, this argument was based upon their untested valuation report, and as the trial judge
observed, this deficiency in valuation “could be remediated by road access” which both
parties agreed to create. Further, the Appellants had benefited over decades from mining
activities on the land and this too would compensate for any perceived valuation differences.
34. As Evans J.A. (as he then was) observed in the case Super Value Food Stores Ltd. and
anor. v. Yvonne Rahming SCCivApp No. 256 of 2017 at paragraph 30:
“It is well established in law that an appellate court will not
interfere with the exercise of a Judge’s discretion unless it is
clearly satisfied that the discretion has been exercised on a
wrong principle and should have been exercised in a contrary
way or that there has been a miscarriage of justice. …”
35. It is my humble view, that given the constraints with regard to the evidence presented, this
exercise of discretion by the trial judge was the best that could be done in the circumstances.
I see no error of principle nor other fault in the exercise of his discretion. Alternatively, the
trial judge’s exercise of discretion was not plainly wrong and ought not to be set aside on
8

appeal (see generally Bahamasair Holdings Ltd. v. Messier Dowty Inc [2018] UKPC 25 at
paragraph 33).
Ground 5
36. This ground alleges that the trial judge failed to consider the acts of waste committed by the
Respondent. Specifically, that the Respondent allowed raw sewage onto the property and thus
caused a depreciation in the value of the land. This, they contend, should have affected his
appointment.
37. This ground is without merit.
38. At paragraph 33 of the Decision, the trial judge devoted 2 ½ pages of consideration to the
valuation report provided by the former defendant, Annamae Woodside. In this analysis the
trial judge detailed all the alleged acts of waste committed on the property and he noted the
conclusion that:
“[33.] …
(10) … over all (sic) conditions warranted a finding of no
recognized environmental conditions due to ‘de minimis’
conditions, … do not present a threat to human health or the
environment and generally would not be the subject of an
enforcement action if brough (sic) to the attention of the
appropriate Government Agencies…”
39. Further, his conclusions that: (1) road access would remediate any disparity in value, and (2)
the decades of usage of the land by Appellants was a relevant factor, were not plainly wrong
and ought not to be set aside on appeal.
Ground 6
40. This ground alleges that the trial judge was wrong to affirm the partition as suggested by
Thompson J 2006 since this suggested partition had been set aside by the Court of Appeal and
affirmed by the Privy Council.
41. This ground is without merit.
42. Both the Court of Appeal and the Privy Council had decided to set aside the suggested
partition of Thompson J on the ground that it was premature. This was because the decision
on a partition should only have been made after a proper consideration of all the
circumstances.
43. Subsequent to the decision of the Court of Appeal and the Privy Council, the parties proceeded
to advance the partition action. They both made tactical decisions on this partition application,
(a) to insist on a partition rather than a sale; (b) to present competing affidavits; (c) to have no
cross examination.
9

44. As such, the trial judge was hamstrung as to his consideration of the facts. He therefore
exercised his discretion to partition the land as had been suggested by Thompson J since 2006
and gave his reasons for this.
45. I find no fault in the exercise of his discretion.
46. In oral submissions, we addressed the question of whether we should remit the matter to the
trial court for a more extensive consideration of the evidence.
47. I am of the humble view that it would be an abuse of process to remit this matter back to the
trial court for reconsideration. This would allow the Appellants a second bite of the cherry, in
the face of the facts that they made tactical decisions (a) to decide on the partition solely on
affidavit evidence (b) without cross-examination (c) in full knowledge of the unduly
protracted litigation spanning decades, over this disputed parcel of land.
CONCLUSION
48. I am of the view that this appeal should be dismissed.
49. Since the Respondent is clearly the successful party on this appeal, I am of the view that it
should have its costs of the appeal.
50. I therefore would order that the Appellants are to pay the Respondent’s costs of this appeal to
be taxed by the Registrar in default of agreement.
_______________________________________
The Honourable Mr. Justice Smith, JA
51. I agree.
_______________________________________
The Honourable Madam Justice Charles, JA
52. I also agree.
_______________________________________
The Honourable Madam Justice Fraser, JA
10

11
judges:
ments - Court of Appeal of the Commonwealth of The Bahamas 

 
 

 
 
 

 
 
 
 Return to Main Page
summary: Civil Appeal – Partition of land — Consent to partition — Affidavit evidence — No cross-examination — Weight of Evidence – Appellate interference with exercise of discretion – Valuation of parcels — Use and enjoyment of land
